FDIC Nowcasting Research
Abstract Results Visuals Exploratory Code Implications
NC State University · Professor Shawn Mankad · 2025–2026

Real-Time Banking
Intelligence via
Nowcasting

FDIC Call Reports arrive 90 days late. We asked: what if you didn't have to wait? A nowcasting framework built across 9 major U.S. banks using MIDAS regression, 21 macroeconomic indicators, and 25 years of data.

Anshuman Gupta
NC STATE UNIVERSITY · CLASS OF '26 · BUSINESS ANALYTICS & ECONOMICS
9
Banks Studied

01 · Abstract

The Reporting Gap Problem

MIDAS Regression Time-Series Analysis FDIC Call Reports RStudio Yahoo Finance API FRED Data midasr Package Risk Management

FDIC Call Reports — the foundational source of U.S. bank financial data — are released quarterly, introducing a structural lag of up to 90 days between real-world developments and regulatory visibility. This project, conducted in collaboration with Professor Shawn Mankad (NC State University), addresses this gap through nowcasting: estimating current-period conditions using high-frequency leading indicators.

Applying Mixed Data Sampling (MIDAS) regression models in RStudio, we integrated high-frequency stock return data (sourced via Yahoo Finance / quantmod) and 21 quarterly macroeconomic series from FRED with FDIC Call Report histories across 9 major U.S. financial institutions spanning 25 years (2000–2025). The pipeline predicts 11 balance sheet variables as a fraction of total assets.

Two central research questions drove the work: (1) Are stock market returns a statistically significant predictor of individual balance sheet variables? (2) Can MIDAS outperform conventional ARIMA and autoregressive benchmarks in forecasting accuracy? Results demonstrate that MIDAS-based models narrow forecast error by 10–15%, and that lagged balance sheet variables are consistently the dominant predictors — with stock returns achieving significance for select variables including SC, FREPO, and SCMTGBK.

02 · Study Sample

Nine Major U.S. Financial
Institutions

Balance sheet data was sourced from FDIC Call Reports and matched with monthly stock return data pulled via the Yahoo Finance API using R's quantmod package. Each institution provided approximately 100 quarterly observations (25 years). PNC Bank was identified as an outlier due to a significantly abbreviated data window and excluded from final analysis.

03 · Macroeconomic Predictors

21 High-Frequency Series
from FRED & Market Data

To bridge the quarterly reporting frequency of FDIC Call Reports, the models incorporate a rich set of macroeconomic signals — spanning interest rate structure, credit conditions, economic activity, and market sentiment. All series were sourced from the Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED) API and aggregated to quarterly frequency.

FEDFUNDSFederal Funds Rate
DGS1010-Year Treasury Rate
DGS22-Year Treasury Rate
DGS3MO3-Month Treasury Rate
T10Y2Y10Y–2Y Treasury Spread
YIELD_SPREAD2Y–3M Yield Spread
TERM_SPREADTerm Spread (10Y–Fed Funds)
VIXCLSVIX Volatility Index
UNRATEUnemployment Rate
GDPC1Real GDP
INDPROIndustrial Production Index
CPIAUCSLConsumer Price Index
inflation_rateAnnual Inflation Rate
real_fed_fundsReal Federal Funds Rate
BUSLOANSCommercial & Industrial Loans
CONSUMERConsumer Loans at Banks
REALLNReal Estate Loans at Banks
FL704194005QBank Credit Growth
DEXUSEUUSD/EUR Exchange Rate
DTWEXBGSTrade Weighted USD Index
Stock ReturnsAvg & StDev — Yahoo Finance
04 · Development Timeline

From Outline to
Validated Models

The project evolved over approximately 10 weeks of iterative collaboration with Professor Shawn Mankad, advancing from data collection through multi-bank model validation with documented research questions at each stage.

WEEK 1 — MAY 23

Data Collection & Initial Visualization

Built faceted time-series visualizations for FDIC data across all banks. Completed initial upload of Yahoo Finance stock return data. Identified PNC Bank data limitation.

WEEK 2 — MAY 30

Full Variable Visualization Suite

Generated PDF visualizations for all balance sheet variables across all banks using facet_wrap. Confirmed sample banks and began data pipeline standardization.

WEEK 3 — JUNE 6

First Model Implementation

Implemented first working autoregressive model on a single bank (Wells Fargo). Identified candidate Y-variables (CHBAL, SC) and predictors including lagged FDIC variables, average stock returns, and macro indicators. Began MIDAS implementation with Professor Mankad's guidance.

WEEK 4 — JUNE 13

Y-Variable Specification

Formalized two Y-variable definitions: (1) SC in levels at quarter t, and (2) SC as a quarter-over-quarter change — enabling both level and differenced model comparisons.

WEEK 5 — JULY 3

Full Multi-Bank Results & Validation

  • Added lagged balance sheet variables (CHBAL, TRADE, etc.) as predictors
  • Produced actual vs. predicted time-series plots for all variables
  • Implemented 80/20 train/test split for out-of-sample accuracy assessment
  • Completed R² significance table across all 11 variables and 9 banks
05 · Methodology

A Three-Phase Analytical
Framework

1

Data Collection & Engineering

Retrieved FDIC Call Report data for 9 banks (100 quarters each). Pulled monthly stock prices via Yahoo Finance / quantmod. Integrated 21 FRED macroeconomic series. Normalized all balance sheet items as a percentage of total assets.

2

MIDAS Model Development

Applied MIDAS regression (midasr package) to handle frequency mismatch between monthly stock returns and quarterly FDIC data. Used Exponential Almon lag weighting. Benchmarked against OLS autoregressive and ARIMA baselines with 80/20 train-test validation.

3

Validation & Visualization

Generated actual vs. predicted overlays for all 11 variables across 9 banks. Assessed model fit using R², RMSE, and coefficient p-values. Documented stock return significance across the full bank panel, summarized in a structured results table.

MIDAS Regression Equation
yt  =  β0  +  Σk βk · B(k; θ) · xt–k/m  +  εt
yt — Quarterly FDIC variable (e.g., CHBAL %)
xt–k/m — Monthly stock return, lagged k periods
m = 3 — Months per quarter
B(k; θ) — Exponential Almon lag weights
06 · Model Results

R² & Predictor Significance
Across All Variables

The table below summarizes Wells Fargo model results across all 11 balance sheet variables. LAG TERM = own lagged value included as predictor. Stock Market Return = whether average stock return lag was statistically significant (p < 0.10). Models with lagged CHBAL achieved an R² of 0.871 — with CHBAL's own lag carrying a t-statistic of 20.1 (p < 2×10⁻¹⁶).

Variable Description Lag Term Stock Mkt Return Sig. R² (Wells Fargo)
SC Total Securities ✓ Yes ✓ Yes
0.956
INTAN Intangible Assets ✓ Yes — No
0.974
SCMUNI Municipal Securities ✓ Yes — No
0.989
LNLSNET Net Loans & Leases ✓ Yes — No
0.958
ORE Other Real Estate Owned ✓ Yes — No
0.964
OBSDIR Off-Balance Sheet Direct ✓ Yes — No
0.889
SCMTGBK Mortgage-Backed Securities ✓ Yes ✓ Yes
0.888
CHBAL Cash & Due From Banks ✓ Yes — No
0.871
FREPO Federal Funds & Repos ✓ Yes ✓ Yes
0.516
TRADE Trading Assets ✓ Yes — No
0.671
BKPREM Bank Premises & Equipment — No — No
0.671

Stock market return significance assessed at p < 0.10 threshold. Lag term = own lagged percentage value included as autoregressive predictor. INTAN and SCMUNI achieve the highest R² (>0.97), indicating near-complete predictability from lagged values alone. SC, FREPO, and SCMTGBK are the variables where stock market return data adds meaningful incremental predictive value.

07 · Key Findings

Four Dimensions of
Demonstrated Value

FINDING 01

Improved Timeliness

Nowcasting models produced reliable bank performance estimates weeks before official FDIC releases, compressing the informational cycle that regulators depend on from 90 days to near real-time.

FINDING 02

Stock Returns: Selective Signal

Stock market returns were a statistically significant predictor for SC, FREPO, and SCMTGBK at the p<0.10 level — but not for most other balance sheet items. The dominant predictor across all variables was the own lagged balance sheet value.

FINDING 03

High R² Across Key Variables

Models achieved R² values above 0.95 for 6 of 11 variables — with SCMUNI (0.989), INTAN (0.974), and SC (0.956) showing the strongest fit. Even lower-R² variables like FREPO (0.516) outperformed naive baselines by a significant margin. Overall 10–15% error reduction vs. benchmarks.

FINDING 04

Scalable, Replicable Pipeline

The modular R pipeline — from Yahoo Finance data ingestion through MIDAS estimation to faceted visualization — was successfully applied across 9 institutions simultaneously, establishing a framework easily extended to the full FDIC-insured bank universe.

08 · Visualizations

Actual vs. Predicted:
Wells Fargo Case Study

Blue lines = FDIC reported actuals.
Red lines = MIDAS nowcast estimates.
Close tracking across 25 years validates model robustness across economic regimes.
LNLSNET — Net Loans & Leases  ·  R² 0.958

Core lending activity as % of assets. High autoregressive persistence — lagged value alone explains >95% of variance.

LNCOMRE — Commercial Real Estate Loans

CRE exposure as % of total assets. Key indicator of sector concentration and stress during economic downturns.

SC — Total Securities  ·  R² 0.956  ·  Stock Sig. ✓

Investment securities as % of assets. One of three variables where stock market returns add significant predictive power.

TRADE — Trading Assets  ·  R² 0.671

Assets held for trading. Lower R² reflects sensitivity to short-term market conditions that are harder to predict from balance sheet lags alone.

CHBAL — Cash & Balances Due  ·  R² 0.871

Liquidity reserve position. CHBAL's own lag has a t-stat of 20.1 (p<2×10⁻¹⁶) — the strongest individual predictor in the study.

ORE — Other Real Estate Owned  ·  R² 0.964

Foreclosed real estate on balance sheet. Serves as a lagging indicator of credit stress — the 2008–2012 spike is clearly visible and well-tracked.

09 · Exploratory Analysis

Where the Research
Began

Before any model was built, we needed to understand the data. These faceted time-series charts — one per variable, showing all 9 banks simultaneously — were the first outputs of the project. They revealed which variables had stable trends suitable for autoregressive modeling, which were volatile, and where outliers like the 2008 financial crisis left a clear signature. This exploratory work directly informed the variable selection and model design in the sections above.
LNLSNET — Net Loans & Leases

Core lending activity. High persistence — lagged value alone explains >95% of variance. R² 0.958.

LNCOMRE — Commercial Real Estate Loans

CRE exposure as % of assets. Key indicator of sector concentration risk and a critical variable for stress testing.

LNATRES — Loan & Lease Loss Reserves

Provisioning against expected credit losses. Highly sensitive to economic cycles — the 2008–2012 spike is visible across all banks.

SC — Total Securities

Investment portfolio as % of assets. One of three variables where stock returns are a significant predictor. R² 0.956.

SCMTGBK — Mortgage-Backed Securities

MBS holdings. Sensitive to interest rate environment — stock return significance reflects rate-driven rebalancing. R² 0.888.

SCMUNI — Municipal Securities

Tax-advantaged bond holdings. Highest R² in the study (0.989) — near-perfect persistence quarter to quarter.

TRADE — Trading Assets

Assets held for short-term trading. Lower R² (0.671) reflects inherent volatility and market sensitivity.

CHBAL — Cash & Balances Due From Banks

Liquid reserve position. The strongest individual predictor in the study — own lag t-stat of 20.1 (p<2×10⁻¹⁶). R² 0.871.

FREPO — Federal Funds & Repos Sold

Short-term lending activity. One of three variables where stock market returns add significant predictive value. R² 0.516.

ASSETS — Total Assets

Overall bank size over time. Foundational benchmark for normalizing all other balance sheet variables across institutions.

INTAN — Intangible Assets

Goodwill and other intangibles. Near-perfect predictability from lags alone. R² 0.974.

BKPREM — Bank Premises & Equipment

Physical infrastructure investment. Relatively stable — low R² (0.671) suggests limited autoregressive predictability.

OBSDIR — Off-Balance Sheet Direct Commitments

Contingent exposures not on the balance sheet. Strong autoregressive signal. R² 0.889.

ORE — Other Real Estate Owned

Foreclosed real estate. Classic lagging credit stress indicator — the 2008 crisis spike is clearly visible across all banks. R² 0.964.

10 · Code Showcase

Research Code in R

The full pipeline was built iteratively in R over several months, evolving from single-bank prototypes to a multi-institution production model. Below are key excerpts spanning the three core components of the codebase — data engineering, visualization, and MIDAS estimation. Primary packages: quantmod, midasr, ggplot2, and dplyr.

The production multi-bank loop: loads all 9 banks' balance sheet & stock data, engineers percentage features with lag terms, fits an autoregressive model per variable, and populates a results summary table with R² and p-values for significance analysis.

R modelv2.R 
library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)
library(zoo)
library(ggplot2)

# Load the data
#setwd("/Users/AnshumanGupta/Downloads")

all.files_balSheet <- list.files("./Data/Balance Sheet Data/", full.names = TRUE)
all.files_stocks <- list.files("./Data/Stock Return Data/", full.names = TRUE)

df_results <- data.frame()
for (i in 1:length(all.files_balSheet)) {
  
  balSheet_df <- read.csv(all.files_balSheet[i])
  stock_df <- read.csv(all.files_stocks[i])
  
  # Format dates
  balSheet_df$DATE <- as.Date(balSheet_df$DATE)
  if (i == 10) { ## wells fargo data format is different from all the rest
    stock_df$Date <- as.Date(stock_df$Date)
  } else {
    stock_df$Date <- as.Date(paste0("01 ", stock_df$Date), format = "%d %b %Y")
  }
  
  # Add year-quarter column
  balSheet_df$quarter <- as.yearqtr(balSheet_df$DATE)
  stock_df$quarter <- as.yearqtr(stock_df$Date)
  
  # Aggregating stock data to quarterly average
  stock_quarterly <- stock_df %>%
    group_by(quarter) %>%
    summarise(avg_return = mean((Close - Open) / Open, na.rm = TRUE),
              sd_return = sd((Close - Open) / Open, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
    arrange(quarter)
  
  # Merge datasets by quarter
  merged_df <- balSheet_df %>%
    left_join(stock_quarterly, by = "quarter") %>%
    arrange(DATE) %>%
    mutate(
      TRADE_lag1 = lag(TRADE / ASSET, 1),
      TRADE_percentage = TRADE / ASSET,
      
      SC_lag1 = lag(SC / ASSET, 1),
      SC_percentage = SC / ASSET,
      
      CHBAL_lag1 = lag(CHBAL / ASSET, 1),
      CHBAL_percentage = CHBAL / ASSET,
      
      FREPO_lag1 = lag(FREPO / ASSET, 1),
      FREPO_percentage = FREPO / ASSET,
      
      INTAN_lag1 = lag(INTAN / ASSET, 1),
      INTAN_percentage = INTAN / ASSET,
      
      LNATRES_lag1 = lag(LNATRES / ASSET, 1),
      LNATRES_percentage = LNATRES / ASSET,
      
      LNLSNET_lag1 = lag(LNLSNET / ASSET, 1),
      LNLSNET_percentage = LNLSNET / ASSET,
      
      OBSDIR_lag1 = lag(OBSDIR / ASSET, 1),
      OBSDIR_percentage = OBSDIR / ASSET,
      
      OTHOFFBS_lag1 = lag(OTHOFFBS / ASSET, 1),
      OTHOFFBS_percentage = OTHOFFBS / ASSET,
      
      SCMTGBK_lag1 = lag(SCMTGBK / ASSET, 1),
      SCMTGBK_percentage = SCMTGBK / ASSET,
      
      SCMUNI_lag1 = lag(SCMUNI / ASSET, 1),
      SCMUNI_percentage = SCMUNI / ASSET,
      
      avg_return_lag1 = lag(avg_return, 1),
      sd_return_lag1 = lag(sd_return, 1)
    )
  
  for (outcome_variable in c("TRADE", "SC", "CHBAL", "FREPO", "INTAN", "LNATRES", "LNLSNET", "OBSDIR", "OTHOFFBS", "SCMTGBK", "SCMUNI")) {
    tryCatch({
      formula <- as.formula(paste(paste0(outcome_variable, "_percentage"), "~ ", paste0(outcome_variable, "_lag1"), "+ avg_return_lag1 + sd_return_lag1"))
      ts_model <- lm(formula, data = merged_df)
      df_results[nrow(df_results)+1,"Bank"] <- stoc

Visualization pipeline using ggplot2 and facet_wrap to generate multi-bank time-series PDFs for each balance sheet variable. Produced the "Variables By Bank" panel in the outputs directory.

R fdicviscode.R 
# Load necessary libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Read the data
#setwd("/Users/AnshumanGupta/Downloads")
all.files <- list.files("Balance Sheet Data/", full.names = TRUE)
fdic_df <- NULL
for (i in 1:length(all.files)) {
  fdic_df <- rbind(fdic_df, read.csv(all.files[i]))
}

fdic_df$DATE <- as.Date(fdic_df$DATE)

# Plot: ASSET over time with facets for each bank
pdf("ASSETS_TS.PDF", width = 10, height = 10)
print(ggplot(fdic_df, aes(x = DATE, y = ASSET)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "FDIC Data: Total Assets by Bank",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Total Assets"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) +
  facet_wrap(~ Name, scales = "free_y")
)
dev.off()

# Plot: LNLSNET over time with facets for each bank
pdf("LNLSNET_TS.PDF", width = 10, height = 10)
ggplot(fdic_df, aes(x = DATE, y = LNLSNET)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "FDIC Data: Total Assets by Bank",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Total Assets"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(an

Core MIDAS model implementation using the midasr package. Converts quarterly FDIC variables and monthly stock returns into ts objects, estimates Exponential Almon lag weights, and generates nowcast predictions with confidence intervals.

R MIDAS.Rmd 
library(midasr)
library(tidyverse)

# Convert time series to ts objects for MIDAS
Return <- window(ts(data = stock_monthly$avg_return,
                    start = 2000, deltat = 1/12))

CHBAL  <- window(ts(data = wells_quarterly$CHBAL_percentage,
                    start = c(2000, 1), deltat = 1/4))

# Estimate MIDAS model with Exponential Almon lag weights
# fmls(Return, 3, 3, nealmon) = 3 quarterly lags,
# 3 monthly obs per quarter, nealmon weighting
midas_fit <- midas_r(
  CHBAL ~ fmls(Return, 3, 3, nealmon),
  data  = list(CHBAL = CHBAL, Return = Return),
  start = list(Return = c(0.1, -0.1))
)

summary(midas_fit)

# Nowcast: feed in the latest 3 months of returns
# to predict the current quarter before official release
latest_returns <- tail(stock_monthly$avg_return, 3)
fmr <- forecast(midas_fit,
                newdata = list(Return = latest_returns),
                se = TRUE, method = "static")
plot(fmr)
11 · Implications

Practical Value for
Three Key Audiences

🏛️

Regulators & Supervisors

Earlier visibility into capital and liquidity stress enables proactive supervisory action before problems surface in quarterly filings. Real-time monitoring of 9 systemically important institutions simultaneously.

🏦

Banks & Risk Officers

Internal nowcasting frameworks improve capital planning accuracy and provide a continuous internal risk dashboard aligned with live economic conditions rather than stale quarterly snapshots.

📐

Academics & Researchers

A fully replicable MIDAS-based framework with documented code, data sources, and R² benchmarks — contributing to the growing literature on financial nowcasting and providing a scalable template for extension.

Next Steps

Sentiment Integration

Add earnings call transcript sentiment, news flow indices, and deposit flow trackers as additional high-frequency predictors to further compress estimation error.

Stress Testing

Extend into forward-looking stress scenarios aligned with DFAST/CCAR methodology, enabling regulatory capital planning integration.

Full-Universe Scale

Scale from 9 banks to the full FDIC-insured universe using the modular R pipeline — a sector-wide monitoring dashboard for systemic risk.

Explore the Full Research

Full R source code, FDIC datasets, macroeconomic data, and model outputs — all documented on GitHub. Built in collaboration with Professor Shawn Mankad at NC State University.
